narrativeText: Two outdoorsmen in northern British Columbia's remote Kakwa Provincial Park chanced on a remarkable find: thousands of dinosaur footprints scattered across a cliff. The dinosaur stomping ground was trampled by theropods, ornithopods, and Tetrapodosaurus back in the Cretaceous, then preserved in stone, only to be discovered some 140 million years later.
notes: Map of British Columbia with Kakwa Provincial Park marked. Photo at bottom left captioned 'Prince George, BC, 2000' shows Garnet Fraser, described as amateur paleontologist and general physician in Prince George, BC. His friend Bryan Monroe first spotted the tracks back in 2000. Illustration of a theropod dinosaur alongside cast. S001 label visible at bottom right.

narrativeText: We can see how different populations of Sitka spruce were adapted to their home locales by studying how they grow once transplanted here in Vancouver. Trees from California, where the growing season is long, get bigger faster, while trees from Alaska grow slowly, but are hardier when it comes to surviving the cold. These differences are ingrained in the trees’ makeup and help them survive across their range.
notes: Top-right label reads: "Sitka spruce (trunk cross-sections) / Picea sitchensis / All specimens grown in Vancouver, BC". Illustration label: "Sitka spruce / Picea sitchensis". Small illustration credit below tree drawing partially legible. Researcher panel: "University of British Columbia, 2010 / Sally Aitken is a professor in the Department of Forest Sciences at UBC. She studies whether BC's native trees can adapt to a warming climate." Map shows trunk cross-section specimens placed along Pacific coast from Alaska to California.

narrativeText: Underfoot in Vancouver's parks and ravines people have found fossils of ferns, conifers, flowering plants, and shells, some of which date as far back as 250 million years. The fossils you see here were found in Vancouver's neighbourhood of Kitsilano.
notes: Three fossil fern specimens displayed. All from Vancouver, BC. First specimen corrected from Dryopteris sp. to Dryopteris weedi; catalog corrected from 2009-5734 to 2009-5724 a, b. Second specimen catalog corrected from 2009-5738 to 2009-5736 a. Eocene age noted on Dryopteris label. Photo credit: Mari Chojiwa. Locality for all: Vancouver, BC.

narrativeText: Aquatic insects spend either part or all of their life cycles in water — or in some cases, skimming along its surface. Dragonflies, water striders, and backswimmers are just a few aquatic insects you’ll likely see at your local pond.
notes: Two-panel shadowbox. Upper panel shows above-water insects; lower panel shows underwater/surface insects. Bulrush specimen at bottom-left labeled: "Bulrush / Typha sp. / June 24, 2010 / Vancouver, BC / No. E.0006". Insects are labeled by common name and family only (no species-level scientific names). S004 label visible at bottom-right with Beaty museum logos.

narrativeText: British Columbia boasts 2,101 species of moths, but only 184 species of butterflies. You’ve probably spotted common butterflies like the Lorquin’s Admiral, tiger swallowtail, and cabbage white flitting around Vancouver. Some BC butterflies, like the Taylor’s checkerspot (Euphydryas editha taylori), are disappearing fast from habitat loss.
notes: Milbert's tortoiseshell: May 7, 2010, Vancouver, BC. Cabbage white: July 7, 2010, Vancouver, BC. Pale tiger swallowtail: June 5, 2010, Oliver, BC. Lorquin's admiral: July 7, 2010, Vancouver, BC. Faun anglewing: June 8, 2010, Vancouver, BC. Taylor's checkerspot illustration credit: "Donald H. Gudehus". Note: label reads "Milton's tortoiseshell" but scientific name is Aglais milberti (transcribed as printed: "Milton's tortoiseshell"). S005 label visible at bottom-right.

narrativeText: Because mosses are so tiny, they can be easy to miss. But in British Columbia, more than 700 different species grow on tree trunks, along rooftops, and even in the cracks of sidewalks. A simple hand lens will reveal a tiny, beautiful plant world right under your feet.
notes: Large line-drawing illustration of Dicranum fuscescens dominates the left side. Three circular inset photographs show moss specimens: two labeled Dicranum scoparium and one labeled Dicranum rugosum. Label at bottom-left reads "Dicranum moss / Dicranum fuscescens". Bottom portion of shadowbox (researcher panel, S006 label) not visible in this image crop.

narrativeText: Animal life got really interesting really fast during the Cambrian. In fact, most animal diversity emerged then. Within five to ten million years — which seems long to you or me, but not to the Earth — all sorts of new creatures evolved. We call this huge increase in diversity the "Cambrian Explosion."
notes: Five Cambrian specimens displayed. Arthropod NULL resolved to Tuzoia sp. (Lower Cambrian, Cranbrook, BC). Archaeocyathid NULL resolved to Coscinocyathus dentoconus and Metacoscinus sp. Sponge previously listed as Vauxia gracilenta corrected to Syringocnema sp. (Lower Cambrian, Caborca, Sonora, Mexico); catalog corrected from 2008-1781 a,b to 2008-1701 a,b. Bottom sponge labeled Archaeocyatha (Ajax Limestone, Lower Cambrian, Beltana, Australia); catalog corrected from 2009-3027 to 2009-5027. Trilobite catalog corrected from 2009-1286-86 to 2008-128a-88 (Middle Cambrian, Utah). Photo credit: Mari Chojiwa.

narrativeText:
Megalodon were gigantic sharks that roamed the oceans for thousands of years. But 2 million years ago, they went extinct. As the Earth got cooler, the warm waters that megalodon needed disappeared, and this may have brought about the end of the giant shark.

Megalodon could grow to 20 metres long — nearly as big as the Blue Whale skeleton here.
notes: Bottom of panel features a size comparison illustration showing megalodon (red) overlaid on a Blue Whale (grey).

species.notes: Echinoidea. Teaching collection. Multiple specimens displayed showing top, bottom, and various sizes.

narrativeText:
The five powerful teeth look like tiny doves, but they're tough enough to crush hard diatoms and even sand grains.

A living sand dollar looks fuzzy because it's covered in tiny spines, which it uses to burrow in sand. Underneath, it has five grooves lined with tube feet and cilia, which carry food to its mouth like a conveyor belt.

Sand dollars in their natural habitat.
notes: Label reads: Sand dollars, Echinoidea, Teaching collection. Small photo shows sand dollars in natural habitat. Inset circle at top right shows the five teeth (Aristotle's lantern). Multiple sand dollar specimens of various sizes displayed.

species.notes: Teaching collection. Large tooth specimen displayed.
Photo of man sitting on fossil restoration of Carcharodon megalodon jaws.

narrativeText:
Megalodons, the biggest sharks that ever lived, roamed the oceans 17 million years ago. A scientific controversy has raged for decades over whether the megalodons are more closely related to great whites or mako sharks. Scientists believe part of the answer is in the animals' teeth. Which of these shark teeth do you think resemble the megalodon's the most?

Man sitting on fossil restoration of Carcharodon megalodon jaws.
notes: Panel labeled S009 in bottom-right corner (appears to be part of a multi-panel megalodon exhibit with S009). Megalodon tooth label reads: Megalodon, Carcharodon megalodon, Teaching collection.

narrativeText: Millions of microscopic algae live symbiotically inside the tissues of coral polyps, giving the animals much of their colour. The algae get nutrients from the waste produced by the coral while playing a role in building the coral's calcium carbonate skeleton. But when corals get stressed, like many do when the ocean warms, they eject their algae and die. This is called "coral bleaching." Corals' single-celled algal symbionts are called zooxanthellae.
notes: Two labeled specimen groups (not three as originally extracted). First label covers two specimens: Tropical corals / Anthozoa / No. MI4215 and No. MI4216. Second label: Tropical coral / Anthozoa / No. MI4061. Microscope inset shows zooxanthellae cells. Live coral photographs also shown (not separate specimens). Previous third NULL/NULL species entry removed as miscount. Images by Todd LaJeunesse.

narrativeText:
For thousands of years, local First Nations peoples have harvested clams, mussels, abalone, and other molluscs to eat. They used the shells in art and clothing, such as carvings, masks, pendants, ceremonial shawls, and as items for trade.

Tusk shells from western Vancouver Island were used as currency by the Yurok of California.
notes: Seven mollusc shell specimens displayed, representing species used by local First Nations peoples.

species.notes: Unionidae. Displayed open showing nacreous interior.
Five buttons made from unionid mussel shell.
Part of periostracum removed to reveal mother of pearl.

narrativeText:
Snails, clams, mussels, and other molluscs need their shells. But collectors often take living molluscs when shells are at their finest. Decades of worldwide trade have promoted overharvesting, especially in tropical areas. This means fewer molluscs as food for other animals and humans, and has led to extinctions and endangered ecosystems.

Land snails are among the most endangered animals on Earth.
notes: Six species displayed illustrating shell trade and overharvesting. Bullmouth helmet has visible cameo-style carving.

species.notes: Growth series of approximately 9 shells arranged in a spiral pattern from small to large.

narrativeText:
Abalones are flattened sea snails. Like all molluscs, they start out as eggs and then hatch into tiny microscopic larvae with a token shell. As snails grow they add new material to the edge of their shells.

The larva of many molluscs, including abalones, swims freely in the water and is called a veliger.
notes: Inset diagram shows a veliger larva at 0.5mm scale. Shells arranged in growth series spiral.

narrativeText: Carrier snails like to decorate. They glue shells and other objects to themselves, probably for extra protection or camouflage. These snails hold each treasure against their shell's growing edge and then secrete calcium carbonate to cement it into place. To test the hold, they gently rock back and forth. They then remain still to allow the shell to bond. Shells placed upside down look empty and unsatisfying to hungry predators.
notes: Five specimens now identified (previously only 3). Three Xenophora pallidula (Japan) with catalog numbers MI4149, MI4056, MI4134. One Xenophora sp. (MI4058). One Xenophora robusta (West coast of Mexico, MI1841). All catalog numbers now resolved. Specimen count increased from 3 to 5.

species.notes: Many cone snail shells displayed in a spiral arrangement and in rows; family Conidae, Marine Invertebrate Collection. Individual species not labeled.

narrativeText: Cone snails' poisonous, barbed harpoons spear prey like fish and worms, paralyzing them. Some cone snail venom is strong enough to kill a person.
notes: Label reads: Cone snails / Conidae / Marine Invertebrate Collection. Illustrations by Derek Tan show cone snail hunting sequence with fish. Numerous cone snail shells displayed in spiral pattern and vertical row. Illustrations credited to Derek Tan.

narrativeText:
Snails are beautiful escape artists. Because speed isn't an option, they've developed different types of shells to help avoid predators. For instance, slippery shells keep crabs from gaining a grip, spiny shells are tricky for fish to bite, narrow openings restrict access to snails' soft parts, periscope-like shells protect extended body parts, and hard shells act like armour against attack.

A conch can jump straight up into the water using its powerful foot, and Turritella can hide up inside its long shell.
notes: Eight species displayed illustrating diverse shell defense strategies.

narrativeText: What do snails, slugs, clams, and squids have in common? Like all molluscs, they come from the same ancestor, which lived more than 500 million years ago. This mollusc "family tree" shows how they're all related.
notes: Phylogenetic tree diagram showing eight mollusc classes: Aplacophora (illustration only), Polyplacophora (Chitons, specimen displayed), Monoplacophora (illustration only), Rostrochonchia (extinct, no specimen), Scaphopoda (Tusk shells, specimen displayed), Bivalvia (Mussels, clams, and cockles, specimen displayed), Gastropoda (Snails and slugs, specimen displayed), Cephalopoda (Squids, octopuses, and cuttlefish, cuttlefish bone displayed). Illustrations by Derek Tan.

narrativeText:
The mantle is a nifty piece of mollusc anatomy. It creates all the different shell shapes you see. The mantle edge is where new shell grows in layers with the help of glands. A mollusc uses its prismatic gland and, when present, the periostracum gland to build the shell's outer layers, while its mantle gland forms the pearly inner layer.

Shells have growth bands that form over time like tree rings.
notes: Includes anatomical cross-section diagram of shell formation showing: Chalky prismatic layer, Pearly nacreous layer, Mantle, Mantle gland, Periostracum gland, Prismatic gland, Skin-like periostracum layer. Two Turbo marmoratus specimens shown to compare periostracum intact vs mother-of-pearl exposed.

species.notes: Multiple sea star specimens of various species displayed; label reads: Sea stars / Asteroidea / Zoology Collection. Individual species not labeled.

narrativeText: The powerful suction of sea stars' tube feet allows these mostly carnivorous animals to tightly grasp their prey — some can even force open clam shells.
notes: Approximately 10-12 sea star specimens of varying sizes, colors, and arm counts displayed. Label: Sea stars / Asteroidea / Zoology Collection. Small illustration of a walrus at bottom left.

species.notes: Five brittle star specimens displayed in separate mounting boxes; label reads: Brittle stars / Ophiuroidea / Stein Collection. Specimens show regenerating arms.

narrativeText:
Our skin heals when we cut it, but imagine extending this ability to regrowing whole fingers, hands — arms even! That's why researchers are busy studying echinoderms like starfish and brittle stars. These animals can regenerate whole chunks of their bodies that get severed.

Someday, research on how brittle stars regrow body parts, like the ones here are doing, might lead to human organ regeneration.
notes: Five brittle star specimens mounted in separate gray boxes, showing various stages of arm regeneration. Label: Brittle stars / Ophiuroidea / Stein Collection.

narrativeText: Bivalves — molluscs with two-part shells, such as clams and oysters — have colonized one of the most prolific habitats on Earth: sandy and muddy water bottoms. By looking closely at the size and strength of a bivalve's shell, siphon, and foot, you can tell whether it burrows beneath the sand, sits on the bottom in moving waters, or attaches to rocks to keep from floating away.
notes: All six bivalve specimens now fully identified. Four previously NULL scientificNames resolved: Malleus albus (white hammer oyster), Euvola ziczac (zigzag scallop), Siliqua patula (Pacific razor clam), Brechites attrahens (watering pot shell). Panopea abrupta common name resolved to Pacific geoduck. Interpretive captions per specimen: pen shell — byssal threads info; hammer oyster — wedged between coral reef rocks; scallop — iceberg method; razor clam — smooth surface for rapid burrowing; watering pot — lives buried in muddy sand; geoduck — siphon burrows to nearly one metre.

narrativeText: You'll find molluscs in coral reefs, sandy shorelines, deep oceans, rainforests, and almost everywhere in between. Estimated at 200,000 species, molluscs may be the second most diverse animal group on the planet, after arthropods. Mollusc evolution has led to amazing variety, from fleshy to shelled, rainbow-coloured to camouflaged, poisonous to harmless, gorgeous to creepy.
notes: Ten mollusc shell specimens displayed showcasing diversity. All labels clearly legible with catalog numbers.

species.commonName: Sea stars, sea urchins, and sea cucumbers

narrativeText: What is an animal? Vertebrates, such as fish, frogs, birds, and humans, are all animals, but so are insects, worms, molluscs, and many more creatures. Animals have to eat to get their energy—they can't get it straight from the sun like plants do. Most (but not all) animals have muscle and nerve cells.
notes: Overview panel of animal phyla. Mix of specimen photographs and illustrations representing 17 phyla. No individual catalog numbers visible.

species.notes: Two specimens: one whole shell, one cross-section showing internal chambers

narrativeText: As a chambered nautilus grows, it closes off "rooms" in its shell and builds newer, bigger ones. By pumping air and fluid into these chambers through tiny tubes, the nautilus can make itself move up and down in the water. With up to 90 tentacles and two simple eyes, the chambered nautilus is related to squids and octopuses.
notes: Two nautilus shell specimens displayed alongside a line drawing illustration of a living nautilus. Catalog numbers listed as No. M14021 and No. M1022. Illustration credit appears to read 'Diane Tuz' or similar.

narrativeText: Today's cephalopods are mostly squishy, which is to say, they don't leave much in the way of bones behind when they die. Take an octopus, for example. After its body decomposes, all that remains is the hard beak this cunning predator uses to eat. Only a few modern cephalopods make hard outer shells like the nautilus does, though many, like squids, have a hard structure inside for support. Ammonites and belemnites were hard-shelled cephalopods that were abundant until they went extinct 65 million years ago.
notes: All six cephalopod specimens now fully identified. Three previously NULL scientificNames resolved: Spirula spirula (ram's horn squid), Coleoidea (belemnite, Eagle Butte, South Dakota), Ammonitida (ammonite). Nautilus sp. corrected to Nautilus pompilius (species level). Octopus specimen is specifically the beak. All catalog numbers now resolved. Photo credit: Mari Chojiwa.

species.notes: Paguroidea; two additional hermit crab specimens, No. MI4166, No. MI4169

narrativeText: Snails build their own shells. Hermit crabs don't. But both need shells to survive. So what's going on? As snails grow, they create larger shells. When they die and leave intact shells behind, hermit crabs adopt these ready-made homes, trading smaller shells for bigger ones as they grow.
notes: Display shows relationship between snail shells and hermit crabs. Diagram shows hermit crab + whelk shell = hermit crab in shell. Multiple hermit crab specimens in various states displayed.

species.description: a brown alga and close relative of Plasmodium

narrativeText: Malaria started out hundreds of millions of years ago as a harmless microscopic alga, which later evolved into the mosquito-borne parasite that plagues us now. Understanding this evolutionary history led to the discovery that malaria still carries plant-like genetic material, and has prompted scientists to search for a treatment based on the herbicides that we use to kill weeds.
notes: Features researcher Patrick Keeling, a professor in UBC's Department of Botany researching the origin and evolution of single-celled protists, including parasites that live inside cells and cause diseases like malaria. Photo credit: Nick Wiebe. University of British Columbia, 2010. Mosquito family name Culicidae and catalog number E.0399 now confirmed via zoom. Chromera velia described as 'a brown alga and close relative of Plasmodium.' Plasmodium vivax micrograph credited to Wellcome Images.

narrativeText: Algae in our oceans, like the tropical rainforests, maintain about one-fifth of the world's oxygen supply. They turn sunlight, carbon dioxide into sugars and oxygen for other organisms to eat and breathe, and they provide important habitat for marine organisms.

narrativeText: The algae below look so similar that they were originally labelled as one species. But DNA evidence has now placed them in three different species. The study of organisms' DNA has shown us that many things that look the same, like these algae, are actually different... ...and many things that look different are in fact the same. The algae below all belong to one species — a newly discovered one, yet to receive a name.

narrativeText: Clayoquot First Nations people used bull kelp, a brown seaweed that grows to more than 60 feet long in a year, for many different purposes. They traditionally used dried kelp for fishing lines, as medicines for children, and even as firecrackers — the dried floats explode when thrown in the fire.
notes: Researcher bio: Dr. Louis Druehl is a professor of marine botany at Simon Fraser University. He researches algal diversity and how it is affected by human disturbances. Simon Fraser University, 2010.

narrativeText: Some bacteria have special powers. Researchers found intestinal bacteria in people in Japan that can digest seaweed carbohydrates that most gut bacteria can't. The researchers think this comes from eating sushi: some time in the past, Japanese gut bacteria stole a gene from another microbe that lives on nori seaweed. Microbes can trade genes the way people can give each other books or tools, a process called "horizontal gene transfer."

notes: Diagram shows horizontal gene transfer from seaweed bacterium to Japanese gut bacterium. Silhouette figures illustrate prevalence. Researcher bio: Jan-Hendrik Hehemann is a marine glycobiologist at the University of Victoria. He coauthored a paper that described this transfer in gut bacteria. University of Victoria, 2010.

narrativeText: Not all skeletons are made of bones. Organisms have independently evolved countless ways of supporting their bodies. Skeletons can be inside or outside the body, jointed or rigid, massive or microscopic, and based on calcium, silicon, or carbon. The fossils of microscopic skeletons help geologists determine the age of rocks.

narrativeText: Lignin, meaning "wood" in Latin, is the stuff that gives plant cell walls the strength to sprout upwards. Scientists thought it evolved to give land plants the strength to hold themselves up in air, and so researchers were surprised to find lignin in a wave-battered red seaweed. This discovery suggests aquatic algae had lignin over a billion years ago, before the ancestors of land plants split off from the red algal line.
notes: Two specimen sheets displayed showing pressed red coralline algae. Researcher bio: Patrick T. Martone is an assistant professor in UBC's Department of Botany. He studies the evolution of macroalgae and the properties of algal cell walls. University of British Columbia, 2009.

narrativeText: Millions of coralline algae, ranging from flat crusts to finely branched forms, live on Vancouver Island's rocky shores. Coralline algae don't grow near Vancouver because our sea water is not salty enough: it's diluted by the Fraser River.

narrativeText: Most coralline algae grow flat on rocks. About 100 million years ago, some corallines began to branch upright into the water, braving the intense currents and crashing waves. These erect corallines produced strong, flexible joints, called genicula, which allow the fronds to bend without breaking.
notes: Central specimen sheet shows erect branching coralline. Three loose specimens (rocks/shell with pink coralline crusts) are displayed around the sheet.

narrativeText: The specimens at left show one of the two forms of liverworts; scientists call it thalloid. The other form, a leafy liverwort, is at right. Unlike mosses and hornworts, liverworts grow fleshy, short-lived sporophytes. Can you see the tiny cups on the second specimen? Inside the cups are gemmae, tiny discs that get splashed out by raindrops and grow into new liverworts.

notes: Illustrated diagram at left shows thalloid liverwort anatomy including archegoniophores, sporophyte, and gemmae cup. Three physical specimens displayed. Diagram labels visible: Archegoniophores, Sporophyte, Gemmae cup.

species.notes: Labeled: June 13, 2011; Point Grey, Vancouver, BC

narrativeText: Hornworts look like black grass. The blades of "grass" are actually reproductive structures; distinctive photosynthetic "horns" that produce spores. Unlike the many thousands of moss species, there are only about 100 known species of hornworts. Hornworts are a type of bryophyte, or non-vascular plant, different from mosses and liverworts.
notes: Illustration of hornwort plant accompanies specimen photo. Catalog number reads No. E.0540.

narrativeText: An easy way to tell if something's a moss is by what it's not. Mosses are bryophytes, or non-vascular plants, which means they don't move water around with roots and stems. Instead, they absorb water and nutrients through their leaves, and they just anchor themselves with root-like rhizoids. Mosses don't have flowers or produce seeds. Instead, they reproduce via spores, fragmentation, and gemmae (single cells) or brood bodies (groups of cells) that break off to form new plants.
notes: Includes moss life cycle diagram with stages: Spores germinate and grow into a new moss plant, Male antheridia, Sperm swims to female, Female archegonia, Fertilized eggs develop into gametophytes and sporophytes, Spore dispersal. Also includes label for 'Cure green seaweed Monostrop' (partially illegible, top left specimen label). One specimen top-left labeled 'Cure green seaweed / Monostrop' — this appears to be from an adjacent or overlapping display and may be misplaced context.

narrativeText: This species of moss absorbs pollutants. The concentrations of heavy metals in this moss have been used to monitor air quality in the Fraser Valley. It's a "bioindicator": an organism that provides information about the conditions in its environment. Isothecium is very common in wooded areas. This specimen was collected from trees lining Main Mall at UBC.
notes: Scientific name partially legible on label; reads 'Isothecium stoloniferum' based on visible characters. Includes close-up photo of moss on tree bark and a photo of tree-lined Main Mall at UBC. Catalog number reads No. E.0171. The bottom-right corner has the S041 identifier but is mostly cut off.

narrativeText: An alga is an aquatic organism that photosynthesizes, but doesn't have roots like a land plant. Some algae are single cells and can swim through the water, while others are big and fastened to the ocean bottom -- like giant kelp, which can be 200 feet long and creates underwater forests.
notes: Multiple algae types shown. Top-left green specimen label partially illegible. Red alga specimen (Phycodrys isabellae) has readable label with catalog No. A.0101. Some labels are small and partially legible.

Multiple dried capsule specimens shown at bottom right

narrativeText: Some mosses have tiny "teeth" at the openings of their capsules to protect their spores. They open and close depending on heat and moisture. New research shows that early mosses lacked these peristome teeth in their capsules, so peristome teeth probably evolved later in moss evolution. The plants used in this research are kept here in the museum. The genus Polytrichum is known for its numerous peristome teeth attached to a central membrane. Spores fall out through the openings between the teeth.
notes: Features researcher profile: Ying Chang, PhD student in UBC's Department of Botany (University of British Columbia, 2010). She uses the DNA of land plants to investigate evolutionary history. Large macro photo credited to Daniel Cotter. Smaller photos credited to Jolene Vroom.

narrativeText: Pale green Oregon beaked moss forms a luxurious carpet on coastal forest floors and is a common moss on the ground in Pacific Spirit Regional Park. Can you see the prominent "beak" it was named for?
notes: Large magnified macro photo of single capsule showing the characteristic beak. Dried specimen displayed at right. Catalog number reads No. E.0366. Photo credit partially legible, appears to read 'Derek Tan'.

narrativeText: This moss looks like little trees, but they're all connected underground. The moss puts out horizontal underground runners, like strawberries. These produce more "trees," allowing the mosses to colonize new space by cloning, a form of asexual reproduction.

narrativeText: Mosses play an important role in rainforest ecosystems. They soak up water and nutrients like a sponge, and they may not release them back into the ecosystem. Mosses also provide shelter and food for many insects and arthropods, both important parts of the food web.

narrativeText: Mosses reproduce by spores or fragmentation, or by using specialized structures called gemmae and brood bodies, which break off from the main plant. Spores are usually transported by wind, but sometimes insects help to move them around. Gemmae, brood bodies, and moss fragments are also distributed by wind or water, or by hitching a ride on animals.

narrativeText: Mosses of the Aleutian Islands were a passion for Wilf Schofield, who spent 15 years collecting there every summer. The Aleutians are special because they used to be part of a land bridge from North America to Asia. Studying the plants there helps us understand how plants came to live where they do today. Wilf Schofield (1927-2008), professor emeritus at UBC, was a celebrated bryologist. To understand and know bryophytes, you had to actually study them in their natural habitat.

narrativeText: As the spore capsules dry out their shape changes from spheres to cylinders, until the round cap pops off and the capsule explodes. Sphagnum mosses shoot their spores up into the air, where the wind blows them to new sites. Their "gun" is a ball-shaped capsule, filled with spores. As the capsule dries out the pressure increases until finally the capsule blows its top, shooting out the spores.

narrativeText: This moss's silvery appearance comes from tiny "hairs" on the tips of the leaves that reflect sunlight and keep the wind from drying it out. Its compact growth form helps keep water in. These adaptations help it colonize dry habitats, such as Vancouver's cement curbsides and bridges. Usually bright green, the leaves of this moss curl up and look brown when it's dry, making it appear "tortured" — like its name. This is another way of conserving moisture.

narrativeText: If there were only girls left on the planet, humans would have something in common with Takakia lepidozioides. Moonwort's underground lifestyle makes it hard for conservation scientists to know if populations are a healthy size.

narrativeText: Delicious food or deadly poison? Common camas and death camas often grow side by side, and look almost the same when they are not flowering. The bulbs of common camas were an important food source for First Nations peoples on the Pacific coast. But the lookalike bulbs of death camas are deadly: a single bulb can kill a human adult. Death camas bulbs contain a poisonous alkaloid chemically similar to strychnine, but more potent.

narrativeText: Nearly all life on Earth relies on energy from sunlight. Plants, algae, and many bacteria harness the sun's rays with pigments: molecules that absorb light. Pigments like chlorophyll use the sun's energy to change water and carbon dioxide into sugars and oxygen, trapping energy in chemical bonds for later use. Most living things that can't live on sunlight still consume products of photosynthesis — by eating other organisms and breathing oxygen.

narrativeText: Tropical corals need coastal mangrove forests. narrativeText: These clover roots might look infected, but the nodules are actually home to beneficial bacteria. Rhizobium "fix" nitrogen by converting nitrogen from the air into a form that plants can use. The plants benefit, and when they die and decompose, they act as natural fertilizer. Nitrogen-fixing plants, including many plants in the legume family, are part of traditional crop rotation systems that enhance soil fertility.

narrativeText: Most oaks are deciduous, which means their leaves turn colour and are shed each year, but some oaks keep their leaves year-round. These are called "live" or "evergreen" oaks, an umbrella term for the various species of oak that keep their leaves. This is an adaptation to very dry environments, where it is costly for a plant to replace all its leaves at once. Live oaks have evolved multiple times in dry climates in North America, Europe, and Asia. Of the more than 500 species of oaks worldwide, about 30 are evergreen or "live," like this Garry oak.

narrativeText: Traditionally known as knitbone, comfrey has long been used to treat broken bones, bruises, and skin irritations. narrativeText: Take this lichen, wrap it around some root vegetables like camas, and bake it in an earth oven for a few days. You'll get a delicious loaf with the consistency of licorice. That's what many First Nations in the BC Interior used to do. They also used it to make clothes and shoes. Researchers have found that the lichen absorbs nutrients from the vegetables that would otherwise be lost during cooking.
notes: Label reads: Wila (Secwepemc name) / Edible horsehair lichen / Bryoria fremontii. Photos show the lichen growing on a tree, and prepared as dark loaves (traditional food preparation). Small text on label partially illegible for catalog number.

narrativeText: Crustose lichens hang on tight, forming splotches of colour over trees, rocks, soil, old antlers, and anything else that stays still long enough. Foliose lichens are leaf-like, often hanging loose or attached by fur-like growths called rhizines. Fruticose lichens are truly three-dimensional. Some species look like tiny shrubs or hang in long strands.
notes: Large multi-section panel showing three lichen growth forms: Crustose, Foliose, and Fruticose, with labeled specimens in each category. 17 specimens total with catalog numbers.

narrativeText: If you discover and describe a new species, you can give it a name. Whenever we refer to the species, even if centuries from now, we would use this name and remember that you described it. Most species of fungi are undescribed—there may be one awaiting discovery in your backyard! R.J. Bandoni and M. Zang described and named this fungus, so we refer to it as Tremella aurantialba Bandoni & Zang.
notes: Top-right caption: Tremella aurantialba (illustration) / Published in R. J. Bandoni and M. Zang, / Transactions of the Mycological... Vol. 81 (2) / 1990, pp. 270-275. Label on left: Basidia (spore producing cell). Illustrated line drawings of fungal structures in background. Bottom-right credit partially legible.

narrativeText: All these mushrooms are edible: maybe you've even feasted on their unique tastes and textures. But did you know that hundreds of edible mushroom species grow here in British Columbia, from oyster mushrooms to morels? But it's easy to make a mistake identifying edible mushrooms, so never go mushroom hunting without an experienced guide. The poisonous ivory funnel mushroom looks like an oyster mushroom. Can you spot three differences between them?
notes: Upper section shows 8 edible mushroom specimens in a bordered display case. Lower section has comparison photos of ivory funnel vs oyster mushroom. Photo credit partially visible: Kent Brothers.

narrativeText: The most common fungal diseases are athlete's foot and ringworm, and they grow only on the surface of the skin, hair, or nails of mammals or the feathers of birds.
notes: Specimen: Athlete's foot fungus (on feathers), labeled as 'Trychophyton sp.' on the exhibit label (note the 'y' spelling on the label vs. standard 'Trichophyton'), January 25, 2011, UBC campus (lab), Vancouver, BC, No. E.0378. Two circular containers show feathers before (left, healthy golden-brown feathers) and after infection (below, degraded/damaged feathers). Caption: 'Feathers before (left) and after infection (below).' Lower text: 'Onychomycosis, or ringworm of the nail, can be caused by the fungus Trichophyton rubrum. It can take four months for the antifungal medicine to cure the disease.' Medicine bottle is from Shoppers Drug Mart: TERBINAFINE 250MG, 'Take ONE tablet(s) daily for 4 months.' Large photo shows an infected human toenail. CORRECTIONS: Previous extraction had NULL common name for Trichophyton rubrum — now resolved as 'Nail ringworm fungus'. Catalog number E.0378 now readable. Medicine details (Terbinafine 250mg) now captured.

narrativeText: Scientists have traditionally studied the lichen by looking at its component parts: the fungus plus the alga or cyanobacterium. But a new approach challenges lichenologists to think of the lichen as a complex, self-sustaining ecosystem—far more than the sum of its parts. Lichens contain producers (algae), consumers (fungi), and decomposers (fungi and bacteria), just like ecosystems.
notes: Multiple lichen specimens displayed vertically with small labels. Most labels are very difficult to read at this image resolution. Bottom section mentions Trevor Goward as curator of UBC's lichen collection and his role encouraging innovative thinking about lichens. Photo credit: University of British Columbia.

narrativeText: Fungi recycle wood, returning nutrients to soil. Hidden inside, they can digest wood until it crumbles. Later, fungi reveal themselves as shelf-like fruiting bodies — conks — that send out spores to infect more wood. Brown lignin remains after brown rot fungi digest cellulose from wood. White rot fungi remove brown lignin in wood, leaving white cellulose.
notes: Shows wood decomposition by fungi. Upper section contrasts brown rot (western hemlock, E.0079) and white rot (bigleaf maple, E.0080) wood samples, both collected June 21, 2010 from University Endowment Lands, Vancouver, BC. Lower section shows conk specimens: artist's conk cross-section (E.0078) and red-belt conk collection (E.0069–E.0076). Multiple small fungal fruiting bodies also visible along the curved display band. Catalog numbers corrected from previous extraction.

narrativeText: Heteroptera's 40,000-plus species worldwide include carnivores like the assassin bug, herbivores like the squash bug, and parasites like the infamous bed bug. Some of these "true bugs," such as water striders and back-swimmers, are aquatic.
notes: Overview display of Heteroptera (true bugs) organized by infraorder: Pentatomomorpha, Nepomorpha, Gerromorpha, Leptopodomorpha, Cimicomorpha. Multiple pinned specimens per family shown. No individual catalog numbers visible.

narrativeText: What makes a bug a true bug? The answer is that all true bugs have special mouthparts for piercing plants or other insects and sucking on their fluids. You might have seen these true bugs in your own backyard. The assassin bug paralyzes its prey by injecting it with toxic saliva.
notes: Includes electron microscope image of assassin bug mouthparts. Five labeled specimens with catalog numbers.

species.notes: June 19, 2007; Vancouver, BC; No. E.0467, No. E.0469

narrativeText: Is your turf all torn up? If birds and skunks are digging through your lawn looking for a tasty snack, your soil is probably infested with European chafer beetle grubs. The first BC sighting was in New Westminster in 2001. This invader is now common across the Lower Mainland. Tall, healthy lawns can fend off the grubs. Infestations can be controlled by applying predatory nematodes, available from large garden centres.
notes: Life cycle diagram of European chafer shown. Adult beetles and larvae displayed as specimens. Photo credit partially visible at bottom left.

narrativeText: Jumping spiders hunt like cats: when they spot prey with their big eyes, they sneak up and pounce. Females also use their big eyes to watch colourful males wooing them with elaborate dances.
notes: Multiple macro photographs of jumping spiders surround the specimens. All photos by Wayne Maddison. One specimen visible in a small container (bark/twig habitat).

narrativeText: Born in rivers, lakes, or ponds, caddisfly larvae are known as "underwater architects" because they build themselves protective cases out of things like gravel, twigs, sand, or other materials held together with strands of silk.
notes: Four caddisfly larval cases displayed with corresponding illustrations showing case architecture. Credit: Artiom Shostak visible at bottom. Small icons in circles show case cross-section patterns.

narrativeText: When you can't run, you hide -- we all know that from our days in the playground. Insects have figured that out too, which is why so many of them have evolved to blend into their surroundings in order to avoid getting eaten by birds and animals.
notes: Text-only panel about insect camouflage. No specimens visible. Label in corner reads S015, suggesting this may be a title/introductory panel for a camouflage-themed display. The file is named s095 but the visible label reads S015.

narrativeText: Like birds, some butterflies and moths make incredible voyages from breeding grounds to overwintering sites each year. Monarch butterflies, some flying a thousand metres above the ground, travel thousands of kilometres, from breeding sites in Canada and the northern United States to overwintering sites in Mexico and California. Each new generation of monarchs knows exactly where to find their ancestors' roosts.
notes: Includes photo of monarchs roosting in trees, credited to Beth Sargent. Specimen listed as from teaching collection with no catalog number.

narrativeText: Brilliant metallic colouring helps some butterflies confuse predators and communicate with each other. Instead of pigments, microscopic structures on their wings create these colours through interference — selectively cancelling out certain light wavelengths and reflecting others, in the same way as oil creates iridescent rainbows on water.
notes: Includes two SEM (scanning electron microscope) images of wing scale structures at upper right, labeled '100PM 10KV 05 003'. No species labels visible for any of the four butterfly specimens.

Displayed in upper right circle and as option E in quiz; also shown in split illustration

narrativeText: Some insects survive by disguising themselves as animals their predators avoid — a strategy called mimicry. For example, hoverflies scare off predators by mimicking the black and yellow stripes of wasps (such as a yellowjacket). Little do the predators know, the flies are harmless! Can you tell which of these insects are not dangerous and are sending a false warning? (Answers are hidden in the illustration above)
notes: Interactive quiz-style exhibit about insect mimicry. Split illustration shows hoverfly (left) vs yellowjacket (right). Specimens appear faded/washed out in photo. Five specimens labeled A through E.

narrativeText: "The Creator, if He exists, has an inordinate fondness for beetles." Attributed to J.B.S. Haldane
notes: Artistic display of diverse beetle specimens arranged as a necklace/chain. Large line drawing of a weevil in background on left. Credit text reads 'Sarah Latonas' at bottom left. Two very large longhorn beetles at center. No individual species labels; no S-number visible in image.

narrativeText: Goliath beetles, from Africa, are the heaviest insects in the world. The Atlas moth, of Southeast Asia, has the largest wingspan. In the past, insects were even bigger. Hold your arms out wide: that’s how big a dragonfly was when dinosaurs walked the earth!
notes: Illustration credit 'Derek Tan' visible. Meganeura illustration is a line drawing, not a specimen.

narrativeText: People eat insects every day in many parts of the world. Grasshoppers, mealworms, giant water bugs, and ants are popular choices. Insects are high in protein and iron, low in fat, cheap to rear, and easy to prepare. Raising a cow costs astronomically more than producing the same amount of insect protein. Now there’s some food for thought!
notes: Includes photo of silkworm larvae for sale in Seoul, Korea (credit Derek Tan). Also shows commercial products: 'Larvets Original Worm Snax BBQ' package and an insect lollipop. Line drawing of various larvae/insects at lower right (credit Sigal Friedmann).

narrativeText: When a dead body is found, forensic entomologists use the insects associated with it to help discover when the person died. If a body is fresh, they’ll find mostly blow flies. But as the corpse ages, insects like house flies and carrion beetles multiply. To establish the time of death, these scientists need to understand insect development and be able to identify species.
notes: Forensic entomology theme. Illustration shows crime scene with three figures and 'POLICE LINE DO NOT CROSS' tape. No catalog numbers visible for any specimens. House flies labeled by family Muscidae rather than genus/species.

species.notes: Family Sarcophagidae; specimens in dashed box lower left
Family Calliphoridae; specimens in dashed box lower left

narrativeText: Carrion beetles love to eat carcasses: gross maybe, but essential work. Adult beetles are drawn to a body by the smell of decay. Only flies get there faster. Once the beetles arrive, they eat fly maggots and carrion, mate, and lay eggs. Carrion beetle larvae feed on fly maggots as well as the carcass, then burrow into the ground to pupate. Adult carrion beetles emerge from underground three weeks later to start the cycle again.
notes: Companion to S102 (forensic entomology). Illustration of animal skull carcass with flies by Artiom Shostak. Carrion beetles grouped under family Silphidae; skin beetles grouped under Dermestidae. Specimens appear in dashed outline boxes. No catalog numbers visible.

narrativeText: The buzz of bees might be hot gossip. Bees and wasps are social, living together and sharing the work. The queen lays eggs fertilized by male drones, and female workers build the nest and care for the young. Drones are short-lived: they die after mating. The cells of the comb house the young bees.

species.notes: Collected at White Lake, Okanagan, BC. Viewed through built-in magnifier lens.

narrativeText: Sometimes the best way to control an invasive species is by introducing a predator — a strategy called biocontrol. Diffuse knapweed has been taking over the grasslands of the BC Interior for decades. Although 12 species of insect herbivores were introduced as biocontrol agents, only after the establishment of the weevil Larinus minutus did UBC scientists observe a dramatic decline in knapweed density. Judith Myers is a professor in UBC's Department of Zoology. She studies biocontrol and insect population cycles.
notes: Photo of Prof. Judith Myers (University of British Columbia, 2011) included in display.

narrativeText: Beetles are the most diverse group of insects in the world. Look for these ground beetles, long-horned beetles, weevils, and ladybird beetles in your backyard or garden.

narrativeText: Most spiders live alone, but about two dozen known species of tropical spiders are social, living in large colonies. They cooperate in maintaining a communal web and caring for the colony's offspring. Working together, they can catch bigger prey than one spider could alone. Leticia Aviles is a professor in UBC's Department of Zoology. She has studied social spiders in Ecuador and Brazil to understand why organisms live in groups and cooperate.
notes: Second photo of Anelosimus domingo shows colony web in Cuyabeno Nature Reserve, Ecuador (photo credit Leticia Aviles). Magnified photo inset shows spiders cooperating on prey. Photo of Prof. Leticia Aviles by Jen Guevara (University of British Columbia, 2010).

narrativeText: If you've ever scratched a bug bite, you know how annoying (and painful!) biting flies can be. Deer flies, horse flies, black flies, mosquitoes, and many other biting flies use piercing mouthparts to feed on human and other animal blood.
notes: Illustrative/diagrammatic display only. Purple line drawings show mouthpart anatomy and cross-sections of skin penetration for each fly type. Three magnifier lenses mounted on display (presumably for viewing actual specimens not visible in photo). No catalog numbers visible.

narrativeText: From pollination to decomposition, flies perform important roles in nearly every ecosystem. Crane flies (Tipulidae) usually don't eat anything as adults, but their larvae often help break down decaying plants. Robber flies (Asilidae) stab and drain insect prey with their beak-like mouthparts. Bee flies (Bombyliidae) look like bumblebees and often pollinate plants.
notes: Background illustration shows enlarged fly head detail.

narrativeText: "If all mankind were to disappear, the world would regenerate back to the rich state of equilibrium that existed ten thousand years ago. If insects were to vanish, the environment would collapse into chaos." — Edward O. Wilson
taxonomyTree: Order Hymenoptera. Superfamilies shown: Tenthredinoidea, Chrysidoidea, Vespoidea, Apoidea, Ichneumonoidea

narrativeText: In the Burgess Shale, in Canada's Rockies, thousands of marine animals were fossilized 505 million years ago. The unusual, detailed preservation of these animals' soft parts offers a rare window into our deepest evolutionary past, displaying ancient ancestors of modern groups alongside unique, extinct lineages. Marella is unique, but shares a common ancestor with arthropods such as trilobites, crustaceans, and horseshoe crabs. This scaly animal could be an ancestor of segmented worms or molluscs — or could belong to an extinct group.
notes: All specimens on loan from the Redpath Museum, Montreal. Large chalk-style illustrations of Marella and a jellyfish-like organism accompany the fossils. Some catalog numbers too small to read reliably.

narrativeText: Anomalocaris hunted the oceans that covered British Columbia 500 million years ago. It took scientists nearly a century to discover what the animal really looked like, because most fossils showed only parts of its body. Which part of Anomalocaris do you think these fossils show?
notes: Large red-brown illustration of full Anomalocaris body. Three fossil specimens displayed across top of panel. Catalog numbers prefixed with No.

narrativeText: The chambered nautilus is one of only a few modern descendants of ancient nautiloids. The nautiloids evolved quickly in the Ordovician to become the ocean's top predator, gobbling up early fish and other sea creatures. Some had coiled shells, others straight. The biggest were a massive 10 metres in length — the largest animal that, until then, had ever lived.
notes: Photo of modern chambered nautilus at top. Three fossil nautiloid specimens displayed. Catalog numbers prefixed with No.

narrativeText: Large coral reefs became more common in the Silurian, a time when marine life flourished and diversified in warm and shallow seas. This wasn't entirely coincidence. Scientists now know that coral reefs are hot spots for new species to evolve.
notes: Large red-pink illustration of tabulate coral structure. Four fossil coral specimens displayed. Illustration credit: Marc Chiappa.

narrativeText: Graptolites are extinct filter-feeding animals that lived from 500 to 350 million years ago. They lived as colonies, their small, soft bodies protected by a common skeleton. Most graptolite fossils show the skeleton flattened like a pancake, preserved as a ghostly carbon film on the rocks. This is what graptolite colonies might have looked like in life.
notes: Upper label: Graptolite / Cyrtograptus sp. / Silurian / No. 2010-7654. Lower label: Graptolite / Cyrtograptus sp. / Silurian / No. 2010-7655. Decorative line drawings of graptolite colonies and marine organisms in background. Illustration credit: Derek Tan. S115 marker at bottom right.

narrativeText: Placoderms were well-protected with armour and spines. Known as "the age of fish," the Devonian had an amazing diversity of fish — both weird and wonderful — that are now extinct. Eusthenopteron was once considered the "missing link" between fish and tetrapods.
notes: Red-brown illustration of a placoderm at top. Illustration credit: David Gillan. Two fossil specimens displayed.

Label reads Rugose Coral, Heliophyllum halli. Devonian. Solitary rugose corals are often called horn corals.

narrativeText: Long-extinct sea creatures left these fossils, but the animals looked and acted like their modern relatives. Like sea anemones and modern corals, these corals fed with tentacles around their mouths. Devonian brittle stars look similar to modern ones and move the same way — using their flexible arms. Solitary rugose corals are often called horn corals. Today there are about 1,500 species of modern brittle stars.
notes: Multiple coral and brittle star fossils displayed. Photo of modern brittle star included. Many labels are small and difficult to read. Catalog numbers not reliably legible.

narrativeText: Big bugs and swampy fern forests characterized the Carboniferous. The name means "coal-bearing," since many beds of coal were formed from these forests.
notes: Four plant fossil specimens displayed. Illustrative silhouettes of Carboniferous plants and a large dragonfly at bottom of panel. Some label text is small.

species.notes: Irati Shale, Lower Permian, São Mateus do Sul, Brazil

narrativeText: Mesosaurus was one of the first reptiles to go back to living in the water where its amphibian ancestors had once lived. Like the modern crocodile, it had nostrils at the top of its jaws and needed to keep its head at the water's surface to breathe. Mesosaurus, which became extinct about 270 million years ago, has been found in Africa and eastern South America—evidence that these two continents were once joined.
notes: Large red illustration of Mesosaurus at top. Map showing Africa, South America, India, Antarctica, Australia with Mesosaurus locations marked. Fossil specimen displayed at lower left.

narrativeText: These leaflets are a fossil of Walchia, an evergreen tree that thrived about 300 million years ago. This tiny animal, Branchiosaurus (not to be mistaken with Brachiosaurus, the biggest dinosaur that ever roamed Earth!), is an early amphibian that lived at the same time as Walchia.
notes: Two fossil slabs displayed. Upper slab shows Walchia leaflet impressions. Lower slab shows Branchiosaurus specimen. Blue tape visible on specimens.

narrativeText: Monotis, or paper clams, lived about 200 million years ago. Their fossils have been found in British Columbia, but also as far away as Austria, the Himalayas, and Nevada. We don't know why they were so widespread, but some hypothesize they might have travelled by attaching themselves to floating masses of seaweed.
notes: Three fossil slabs with Monotis clam impressions displayed. Decorative scallop shell illustrations in background. Catalog numbers now resolved: 2010-7609, 2010-7610, and 2010-7608 (third partially cut off at edge, from Peace River, Alberta). Artist credit: Mari Chijiiwa.

Lower Jurassic, Nürtingen, Germany; multiple specimens in tray

narrativeText: Fossils from heaven and hell? These fascinating fossils have puzzled and enchanted people for thousands of years. Fossil echinoids are sometimes known as "fairy loaves" because of their resemblance to a round loaf. Keep one in your home and you will always have bread. English folklore tells of "thunderbolts" (belemnites) thrown down from the heavens during storms and "devil's toenails" (Gryphaea) cast off the devil himself.
notes: Multiple fossil types displayed illustrating folklore associations. Three echinoids at center, belemnite specimens at left (single and tray of five), Gryphaea specimens at lower right with blue tape.

narrativeText: Even though insects are everywhere, and we think of them as hardy, it's rare for them to be fossilized, since their outer skeleton, known as an exoskeleton, is actually quite delicate. The oldest known fossilized insect is about 400 million years old, making insects among the first animals to live on land.
notes: Large granite slab with insect fossil at top center (2008-524 a, Florissant tuff, Oligocene, Florissant, Colorado). Second smaller slab nearby (2008-524 b, same locality). Lower specimens: Insect from Quaternary, Smithers, British Columbia (2008-128a-115); Two insects from Tertiary, Florissant, Colorado (2008-128a-205); Ant from Florissant shale, Miocene, High Park, Colorado (2009-4900); Fly from Florissant shale, Eocene, High Park, Colorado (2008-2297 a); Corixa pullus from Miocene, Bonn, Germany (2009-4981). Handwritten numbers P 595 and P 616 visible on specimen stones. Artist credit: Derek Tan. Major corrections from previous extraction: scientific name was 'Corisa pulchra' corrected to 'Corixa pullus'; multiple catalog numbers were misread.

narrativeText: Phareodus testis is an extinct fish species that lived approximately 50 million years ago. This fossil is from the Green River Formation in Wyoming where periodic blooms of blue-green algae caused low oxygen levels in the river, suffocating the fish. As they sank to the bottom they were gradually covered in fine-grained limestone mud. Repeated episodes over millions of years created layers of some of the world's best-preserved fossils. Phareodus testis has some distant living relatives today, including the goldeye found in northern Canada, and several types of freshwater arowana fish.
notes: Large complete fish fossil displayed. Red fish outline illustrations in background. Map of North America with Green River Formation location marked. Handwritten label on specimen reads partially: 'Approved for personal display' and additional text.

species.notes: Copy of original herbarium sheet; Rosa Curls, West Kettle River Valley, BC

narrativeText: "Rosa ×engelmannii subsp. britannicae-columbiae is named for the province of British Columbia, where my early Rosa education at the University of British Columbia led to my lifetime study of Rosa in North America." -Dr Walter H. Lewis, pictured with Dr. Margery Eliot-Lewis in the Beaty Biodiversity Museum in 2016. The British Columbia rose was found within our collections and named as a new hybrid in 2016 by rose expert and UBC alumnus, Dr. Walter H. Lewis. This specimen was collected in 1953 and first identified as our common Rosa woodsii with the collector knowing the name didn't quite fit the plant's morphology. Now, DNA technology has revealed this species' complex parental combination as a hybrid new to science.
notes: Herbarium sheet (copy of original) displayed with pressed rose specimen. Photo of Dr. Walter H. Lewis and Dr. Margery Eliot-Lewis inset. Rose flower photograph inset. Green leaf illustrations in background. Yellow callout box mentions holotype definition.

narrativeText: Underfoot in Vancouver's parks and ravines people have found fossils of ferns, conifers, flowering plants, and shells, some of which date as far back as 55 million years. The fossils you see here were found in Vancouver's neighbourhood of Kitsilano.
notes: Three fossil fern specimens displayed with red fern frond illustrations in background. Labels identify each specimen with location as Vancouver, BC. No shadowbox number marker visible in image.

species.notes: Genus of moss that was the focus of Dr. Horton's research; several type specimens described by Dr. Horton are referenced; specimen packets and a petri dish with moss visible

narrativeText: THE DIANA G. HORTON AND DAVID W. JAMIESON COLLECTIONS. Many of Dr. Hortons specimens are Encalypta, the genus of moss her research focused on. These include several type specimens described by Dr. Horton. Types are natural history specimens scientists use to describe species new to science. From the South Shetlands to Svalbard, thousands of the specimens in the Beaty Biodiversity Museum's bryophyte collection come from bryologists Dr. Diana G. Horton and Dr. David W. Jamieson, generously donated by Dr. Hortons sister, Patricia Webb. This world-class collection contains bryophytes collected from over 80 countries and territories across all seven continents, from both Dr. Hortons extensive collecting and exchange of specimens with researchers and institutions around the world.

Labels on individual packets are partially illegible at this resolution.

narrativeText: Starting in 2004, mycologist Oluna Ceska conducted an almost-15-year survey of the fungi of Observatory Hill in Saanich, British Columbia. During her 40-50 visits each year, Oluna found around 1,450 species of fungi, many of which she collected and are now housed here at the Beaty Biodiversity Museum. Scientists estimate that there are one million species of fungi around the world which remain unknown to science; 10,000 of which are located in BC. The work of collectors such as Oluna Ceska is incredibly valuable to scientists working to identify these unknown species. In recognition of Oluna's extensive collections and contributions to Canadian mycology, two species she discovered at Observatory Hill were named after her: Inocybe ceskae and Cortinarius ceskarumfceskae.

narrativeText: Natural history collections can predict the effects of global climate change. Researchers can use pressed plant specimens to compare the flowering date with annual temperature records. Scientists were able to determine plants at higher elevations and those which bloom earlier in the season are more sensitive to warming temperatures. Thimbleberry blooms 4.4 days earlier for every degree of warming. Barbara Neto-Bradley, one of the scientists behind this research, completed her MSc in Botany in the Whitton and Pennell labs at UBC's Biodiversity Research Centre. Listen to Barbara talk about some more work on leaf shape and plant evolution here: https://explore.beatymuseum.ubc.ca/researchers-revealed/b_neto-bradley/q/ Linda P.J. Lipsen, another scientist on this study, is the Vascular Plant and Algae Collections Curator at the Beaty Biodiversity Museum. You can learn more about pressed plants and their importance in research here: https://explore.beatymuseum.ubc.ca/pressed-plants/q/ Kopp, C.W., Neto-Bradley, B., Lipsen, L.P.J., Jas, S., & Smith, S. (2020). Herbarium records indicate variation in bloom-time sensitivity to temperature across a geographically diverse region. International Journal of Biometeorology, 64(5), 873-880. doi: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00484-020-01877-1

narrativeText: Huber Moore was a true community scientist and a unique collector of nature. Originally from Milton, Ontario, his father was a farmer and taught Huber the local birds and plants, sparking Huber's interest in the natural world. He had a keen interest in birds, butterflies, vascular plants, and mosses and had a unique way of documenting and storing his collections. His collection exhibits the importance of community collectors for science and art. With no formal education in sciences, his work shows that higher education is not necessary to be involved and make an impact in the scientific community and conservation efforts. Our goal is to showcase his unique collection and make it accessible for scientists, artists, and all those who may be inspired by his life's work. Learn more about the Huber Moore collection here. To leave feedback, email info@beatymuseum.ubc.ca
